Um, so growthx is, um, it's at its heart, uh, agency model.
Um, so we are, uh, marketing agency in the way that we operate right now, but, um, we do have an internal product that we, uh, leverage, um, to create all of our content.
So, um, we only produce like SEO content, blog content.
We do some thought leadership, but like, we're not in like social market, like paid ads, like video, we video is like its own thing that we're not like.
It's not our bread and butter.

So, like, core SEO content marketing and our platform that we've built basically leverages different models throughout a workflow to do deep research, bring in, like, context from artifacts that we've built, like, fact check.

Think about brand and tone from documents that the client has given us, like, pull in keywords based on research that we've done, and then generate an article that, like, kind of matches with the roadmap that we've built for clients.
So that is all done automatically, but we really enforce human in the loop.
So, like, you might have noticed that throughout that, I was like, we pull in these things.
So, like, we're doing a lot of, like, we are doing all of the initial, like, gap analysis.
We're working really closely with the client to start to get a lot of company context.
of context.
So, like, And then...
And as we work through the process, there is a manual reading and editing process at the end of every article that we produce before we publish.
Or most articles, when we get to programmatic, there's a little bit less of that.
And that's where the managing editor comes in.
So we work in a pod model, and we have content managers that actually run the workflows.
Like, they press play, they do a first read-through, make sure that, like, the keywords are right, and kind of read for tone and stuff.
Managing editor will manage three to five accounts at this point.
And each account is generating five-ish pieces of content a week.
So reading and editing that content, just making sure it all looks good, kind of a little bit of, like, tastemaker and alignment there.
And then if you're working with...
The client that has, like, more programmatic, which would be where we're, like, really pushing through, like, more automated content.

There's also, like, some work with the pipeline itself, like the workflow itself, thinking about kind of what are the inputs that we can affect to improve the outputs and, like, the end result of the article.
So, you know, that's two pieces.

So editorial is the managing editor, also a little bit of, like, that technical ability, and really it's less like you're manipulating the code or doing anything like that, and more can you translate what's working and not working in the content into an explanation that an engineer or product person would understand to improve the, like, baseline product that's generating all of our And then the last piece is, like, project management, since you are managing multiple clients.

So the technical managing editor is not necessarily setting strategy and like having renewal conversations or anything like that, but it is a little bit of a project management role where you're saying, okay, are we on track to deliver everything that we promised this week, this month?

You know, our contracts are based on number of deliverables per month, so, you know, how are we meeting those goals, what are the numbers look like in terms of performance, do we need to tweak anything, and then you're working with an account director, an account strategist, that can help think like bigger picture, because it's hard to kind of be in the weeds, and take that step back.

Now, with AI in the loop, it's easier to create content, but it's just as hard to create good content now as it was before AI happened.

Your editor-in-chief, was it Matt that I talked to before?
I think so.

So the thing I absolutely hate is when a client comes back to you and says, this isn't working.

Because I think, okay, then let's try to make it work for you.
What can we do to do that?
I love working with people.

Could we try this?
How about we try this?
And then talking to the writers and saying, hey, can we switch this around a little bit?
Because the client is looking for this.
As long as that line of communication is open, this is absolutely the kind of job that I excel at.

Can you talk to me a little bit about, like, more about your AI experience specifically, and like how you've worked with AI and generating content?
Sure.
So I have only, I've had AI experience in the last three years with the company that I was at formerly, which is Code and SQL.
They were brand new to it.
They didn't understand.
was right at the beginning of companies adopting AI on a scaled size.
And what we did was I brought AI in from the beginning.
I told them this is something that actually absolutely has to have governance on it.
Because what I realized in writing, they wrote very technical, high-end niche articles about legal compliance.
And I told them, we have to look at the funnel and see who's reading this, who do you want to bring in, how we can write this to hit a more general audience.
And AI was perfect for that.
However, after our first few experiences with AI, I warned them that this sounded like garbage.
It sounded like every other piece of content out there.
And of course, that's what AI is.
It just goes out and looks for other pieces of content that are similar, and it gives you suggestions of what you should write.
Instead, what I use AI for, which I find it to be very helpful, is to help me outline ideas, to help me research the cadence of how something is written.
So I can read something.
And as an editor, again, I've had many, many years of editorial experience, I've worked in newsrooms, I've worked in magazines, and I know that that editing process is probably the most important.
There are no great writers, there are only really fantastic editors, and then good writers after that.
But the editing part of that, as far as AI went, was helpful only in telling me, does this sound right?
Does it connect to the voice?
Here's a website, look at this website and tell me, is their voice and their tone consistent with what What this client is trying to prove?
I also ask before anything is written, AI or otherwise, that you have to answer three questions.
What is this content for?
Who's the audience you're aiming for?
And what are they going to get when they walk away from this thing?
If you can't answer any of those correctly, AI or not, you're not going to have, you're not going to achieve your goal with that.
But I like the fact that I can use AI to kind of coagulate my thoughts, you know, like I'll say, here's 1500 words, and I'll look at it, and I'll read it.
And I'll think there's something just not right with this.
And I'll ask AI.
Can you give me some suggestions, how you would improve this, where you would improve this, don't make the changes, just give me the suggestions so that I can put them into my own words.
And that has been, I'm telling you, AI has been a huge, huge denominator in everything that I do now.
Yeah, awesome.
You mentioned that at Animals, you've managed multiple clients at once.
Can you talk to me a little bit about your project management organization style, what tools you use, things like that?
Yeah.

So in the last job that I had for the last three years, before, after the agency work, I used, which I found to be a gigantic help, was Monday.com to kind of organize the boards, make sure everything was where it's supposed to be, get reminders of when things were due and stay on top of those reminders.
was extremely helpful.
When I worked with the animals in the agency, they used Airtable, and they used, I can't remember what the other program they used was, they used Airtable and something else.
Oh, and they used Airtable, a combination of Airtable and Asana, which was fantastic as well, but because that agency...
We handled multiple, multiple clients.
I found it kind of confusing.
And I always asked for my projects to be separated from the general projects just so that I could make sure that I wasn't checking somebody else's work.
I was actually checking what I was supposed to do.
And that was helpful in that.
I also find that it's really great to just keep my own spreadsheets on things and say, you know, you have this on this date.
This is what they're looking for.
then kind of go through that and maybe put that into another productivity program.
But I rarely go cold turkey and just do it by notes.
I'm not going to call a client up and say, tell me what you want, and then take notes on that and hope that that's going to be okay.
All of that stuff has to be mapped out.
So, yes, monday.com was huge.
Airtable was another great enumerator for me.
And then when I worked for B&H Photo, and that's when I led a department of almost 100 people, they used their own proprietary system, a DES system, they called it.
And that thing was, because it was custom built.
It
This, in most cases, worked well for them, but it would have been useless outside of that environment.

Can you talk to me about a time when you've had to quickly shift, like, content strategy for a client?
You know, maybe they, we work with a lot of startups.
We actually, I was just on a call where somebody was like, they said that they wanted one thing, but they're like, right before launch, panicking, and they need another.
So talk to me about how you approach that, and how you think about that shift.
Sure.
Unfortunately, it happens a lot.
I mean, I have never had a job where that didn't happen, but I will tell you specifically, in the last three years, when I was at Cogency Global, most recent one, we got that all the time.
So I worked with subject matter experts who were top of their field, and they wrote their own articles, and all I did was go through them, make sure style, and tone, and voice were the same.
But there were times when they said, you know what, new legislation came out yesterday, and we have to come.
Completely shift the go-to-market campaign for this thing that we're selling.
My thing is, and again, like I said, I worked in a newsroom, so I am accustomed to that kind of chaos.
But my thing is, okay, this thing is going to be shuttled now.
As for this new thing, what do you need?
When do you need it?
What specifics of this can you give me?
And then leave me alone to just get it done.
Because I really, at that point, if they're still clarifying, and again, this happens a lot with startups, they clarify on the run, on the go.
They'll say, okay, today is not yesterday, and now we've changed our mind, we have to do this.
My thing is to just write that all out.
Get from the client what they want, present to them what they ask for.
If they say, well, we've changed our mind, it's something else.
You go right back to the drawing line and say, okay, here's what else, tell me what else you want, and here's what else we can go for.
I can offer suggestions for that, but especially with startups, I'd rather that they suggest it so that they understand this is what they wanted, not what I'm suggesting that it happens.
Because it's too easy to blame the writer then, or the content creators, to say, well, you said this and now this isn't working.
I would rather say, you asked for this, and here's where we're going to go with that.
Here's where I think we should go with that, and how that should work.
Yeah, yeah.
I think that's something that we think about a lot, in terms of like, we spend a lot of time early on in an engagement on calibration, because we want them to be telling us as much as possible.
Because sometimes there's a little bit of like, pointing at each other, like the Batman, or Spider-Man meme.
